channel.title: Landscape design on xpsong
channel.description: Recent content in Landscape design on xpsong

item.title: Analysing spatial patterns of the landscape
item.description:
In landscape ecology, we study interactions between organisms and their surrounding environment. The physical landscape is often quantified using “landscape metrics”, for further analyses alongside other sources of data.

My recent lectures are focused on introducing such concepts to landscape architects. While I’ve used FRAGSTATS and other GIS software for teaching in the past, I’ve always struggled with compatibility/licensing issues that just made the whole process really cumbersome.

Recently, I came across this R package called landscapemetrics. I was absolutely blown away by the sheer power of this thing. I’ve always wanted to introduce people to spatial analysis in R, and to show how a seamless, reproducible workflow in R can be an excellent alternative to conventional software. This was the tipping point for me… I decided to give it a shot!

This post provides a quick preview of the workshop content. The workshop is meant to be a follow-up lesson to the Introduction to R series I’ll be conducting next semester.

Workshop outline:


  1. Why analyse spatial patterns?

  2. Landscape ecology: Conceptual models

  3. Land cover classification

  4. Landscape metrics


I’ve uploaded the slides and data on Github, so that anyone interested to try out the analysis for themselves will be able to do so. If you’re interested in geospatial analysis, check it out!



Slide deck:

Note: Click on the slides and use arrows to navigate (tap on edges if using mobile device)

item.title: An ecosystem service design loop for using vegetation to mitigate the urban heat island effect
Ecological Landscapes in the Development of Kashiwanoha Smart City
item.description:
This design project was part of a workshop held from 17–25 Feb 2017 at the Center for Environment, Health and Field Sciences, Chiba University, Japan. It was conducted as part of a collaboration between Tsinghua University (China), National University of Singapore (Singapore), and Chiba University (Japan).

The design objectives were to:

  1. Assess site conditions in the context of the ‘Kashiwanoha Smart City’ concept and vision. This included the current coverage and distribution of green spaces, how such spaces were used (or not used), environmental constraints faced in urban greening, the needs of people, relationships between the site and adjacent neighbourhoods, etc.

  2. Identify and articulate key issues that can be addressed through planning and design, keeping in mind current encumbrances.

  3. Develop a conceptual design for how green spaces can be improved, added or amalgamated to deliver more functions, either in improving environmental performance (biophysical or biodiversity), or to better meet the needs of people.

 

Team members: Song Xiao Ping, Hiroshi Ichikawa, Ye Jing, Liu Yuehua, Pang Yu Rong

item.title: Ecosystem services in urban landscapes
item.description:
Urban greenery can help mitigate the negative impacts of urbanisation, and improve city liveability for urban dwellers. The benefits associated with greenery are realised through the provision of ‘ecosystem services’ such as evaporative cooling, rainfall interception, and green spaces for recreation.

Most research on urban ecosystem services has been conducted in temperate Europe and North America, but we know relatively little about how tropical cities can be designed to enhance ecosystem service provision. Tropical areas, particularly Southeast Asia, are rapidly becoming more urban, with cities growing larger and people living in higher densities. It is therefore important to understand how urban greenery can contribute to enhancing the sustainability and resilience of such cities.

This project uses new types of data and modelling approaches to understand how we can improve the ecological performance of urban greenery. Physical, ecological and socio-cultural parameters are used as inputs to model various types of ecosystem services. Landscape design is performed using 3D point cloud data, to achieve highly accurate representations of existing landscapes. The iterative process of model simulation and design feedback provides opportunities to improve designs based on scientific results.
